title: Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku: Guardianship Through Technology / Te Kaitiakitanga mā te Hangarau
subject: Technology
culturalContext: Students will investigate the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a significant waterway flowing through our rohe that holds cultural importance to Ngāti Kahungunu. The stream's name references the native kotukutuku (tree fuchsia) that once flourished along its banks, and its current state reflects both historical land use and contemporary environmental pressures.
learningObjectives: Design and construct a simple water quality testing device using basic materials to measure at least two indicators (e.g., turbidity, pH) in Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.
Analyze collected water quality data to evaluate the environmental impact of a specific land use practice (e.g., farming, urban runoff) observed near the stream.
Propose a technology-based solution for a kaitiakitanga (guardianship) challenge identified during the investigation, justifying how it respects both environmental and cultural values.
Present findings and proposed solution to the class, effectively communicating the technological and cultural considerations of the project.

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki te Awa (Connecting to the Stream)
activities.description: Begin with a karakia and waiata to show respect for the awa. A local kaumātua or representative from Mana Whenua shares the pūrākau (story) and significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Students make initial observations and sketches of the stream and its immediate environment, noting potential sources of influence.

activities.title: Hangaia tō Mīhini Whakamātautau (Build Your Testing Machine)
activities.description: In small groups, students apply technological principles to design and build a functional water testing tool. Examples include a Secchi disk for turbidity from recycled materials, or a simple pH testing station using cabbage water indicator. The focus is on functionality, reliability, and suitability for the task.

activities.title: He Aha te Mātauranga? (What is the Knowledge?)
activities.description: Groups visit a pre-determined, safe site along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku to collect water samples and data using their devices. They also conduct a visual riparian zone survey, identifying native/exotic plant species and signs of bank erosion. Data is recorded in field journals.

activities.title: Te Tātari me te Whakatau (Analysis and Decision Making)
activities.description: Back in the classroom, groups analyze their data, comparing results and identifying trends. They discuss the potential environmental impacts their data suggests. Using a 'think-pair-share' approach, they brainstorm a simple technological intervention (e.g., a designed planting strategy, a model for a silt trap, an educational app concept) that could help address an identified issue.

activities.title: Te Whakaaturanga (The Presentation)
activities.description: Groups create a concise digital or physical presentation (e.g., a slideshow, a poster, a model) outlining their investigation process, findings, and proposed technological solution. They must explain how their solution demonstrates kaitiakitanga and consider its potential benefits and limitations.

resources: A local kaumātua or representative from Ngāti Kahungunu iwi to share knowledge
Hawke's Bay Regional Council's 'State of the Environment' monitoring data and maps
Physical materials for prototype construction (recycled plastics, mesh, string, wood, etc.)
NIWA's 'River & Stream Monitoring' guides and digital resources

tasks: Submitted field journal with observations, sketches, and raw data
A group presentation outlining their findings and proposed technological solution
A brief individual reflection on their understanding of kaitiakitanga in technology

nzcAlignment: Technological Practice: Planning for practice, Brief development, Outcome development and evaluation
Nature of Technology: Characteristics of technology, Characteristics of technological outcomes
Key Competencies: Thinking, Using language, symbols, and texts, Participating and contributing
Science: Living World - Ecology, Planet Earth and Beyond - Interacting systems
